one_line_takeaway: A CVPR 2026 HCI/RL paper -- not an AI-security paper -- that trains a lightweight CNN to predict EEG-derived P3/ERP 'cognitive load' signals from driving scene images and folds that prediction into an RL reward, improving CARLA driving-score/route-completion/infraction metrics over TD3, BC, PHIL, standard preference-based RLHF, and TD3-lag baselines in emergency-braking and left-turn scenarios.
key_finding: ERP peak latency correlated significantly with driver reaction time (Pearson, p=0.0438), and ERP amplitude was significantly larger (300-500 ms window, permutation test with 10,000 iterations) when subjects actively reacted to sudden braking versus a passive baseline. The image-based ERP predictor achieved 82% mean 5-fold cross-validation accuracy (per-fold: 80/85/77/86/81), comparable to ResNet-18 (81%), Swin-ViT (79%), and ConvNeXt (80%), but ran at 204 fps versus 73/62/74 fps respectively, saving 2.1 hours of RL training time per 1M steps compared to ResNet-18. Incorporating this predicted cognitive reward into TD3 training ('Ours') outperformed all baselines (Vanilla TD3, Behavior Cloning, PHIL, standard human-preference RLHF, and TD3-lag) on both scenarios: in emergency braking, Ours reached Route Completion 85+/-43, Driving Score 79+/-31, Infraction 0.84+/-0.14, versus the next-best baseline (RLHF) at 73+/-32 / 66+/-39 / 0.80+/-0.19; in the left-turn scenario, Ours reached Route Completion 67+/-8, Driving Score 57+/-10, Infraction 0.77+/-0.05, versus RLHF's 63+/-21 / 49+/-28 / 0.71+/-0.15. Attention visualization also showed the cognitive-reward policy consistently focused on the lead vehicle, unlike more dispersed baseline attention.
core_contribution: The paper proposes an EEG-guided reward-modeling framework for reinforcement learning (RL) in autonomous driving. It (1) collects a new multimodal dataset (EEG, eye-tracking, vehicle control, and semantic-segmentation scene images) from human subjects driving in a VR/CARLA simulator, (2) trains a lightweight CNN to predict, from scene images alone, whether a driving moment would elicit a strong event-related potential (ERP, specifically the P3 component) in the driver's EEG -- eliminating the need to record EEG at inference time -- and (3) integrates this predicted 'cognitive reward' as a negative-weighted term into an RL reward function (alongside collision, idle, and following-gap terms) trained with TD3, and evaluates the resulting policy in two CARLA scenarios (emergency braking and left turn).
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a human-computer-interaction / affective-computing / RL paper about aligning autonomous-vehicle control with human cognitive (neural) signals; it contains no adversary, attack, defense, or security threat model of any kind. Forcing a security frame onto it would misrepresent the paper.
limitations: The study explicitly notes: only two scenarios and 20 participants were used (others excluded for VR motion sickness), limiting data diversity; the EEG feature prediction model is scenario-specific and not shown to generalize across driving contexts; and the approach still requires an initial EEG data-collection phase (even though not needed at inference), so scaling to new tasks/scenarios would require new EEG recordings. The reported standard deviations on Route Completion/Driving Score/Infraction (e.g. +/-43, +/-39) are large relative to the means, indicating high variance across the 5 random seeds not deeply discussed by the authors.

datasets_used: Authors' own new multimodal driving dataset: 20 participants (of 32 recruited), EEG (64-channel Synamps2, 1000 Hz), eye-tracking, active vehicle control, and RGB/depth/semantic-segmentation camera views, collected via HTC Vive Pro Eye VR + CARLA + Logitech G923, across emergency-braking and left-turn scenarios
CARLA simulator towns used for train/test split: Town07->Town04 (emergency braking), Town01->Town05 (left turn)
quantitative_results: EEG feature prediction model: mean 5-fold CV accuracy 82% (folds 80/85/77/86/81), 204 fps
Baseline EEG predictors: ResNet-18 81% acc / 73 fps; Swin-ViT 79% acc / 62 fps; ConvNeXt 80% acc / 74 fps
Training-time savings: 2.1 hours saved per 1M RL training steps vs. ResNet-18-based predictor
ERP peak latency vs. reaction time: Pearson correlation p = 0.0438
ERP amplitude (active vs passive baseline): significant difference in 300-500 ms window, permutation test, 10,000 iterations
Emergency Braking scenario (Ours vs best baseline RLHF): Route Completion 85+/-43 vs 73+/-32; Driving Score 79+/-31 vs 66+/-39; Infraction 0.84+/-0.14 vs 0.80+/-0.19
Left Turn scenario (Ours vs best baseline RLHF): Route Completion 67+/-8 vs 63+/-21; Driving Score 57+/-10 vs 49+/-28; Infraction 0.77+/-0.05 vs 0.71+/-0.15
baselines_compared: ResNet-18, Swin-ViT, ConvNeXt (for EEG/ERP feature prediction from images)

Vanilla TD3, Behavior Cloning (BC), Prioritized Human-in-the-Loop RL (PHIL), standard preference-based RLHF (Bradley-Terry, human pairwise video comparisons), TD3-lag (Lagrangian safe-RL) (for driving policy evaluation)
